meta.title: E84 Engine Replacement Cost UK — Compare Quotes
meta.description: Every BMW X1 E84 engine — N46, N20, N52, N55, N47 diesel. Real UK costs and honest reliability data. Get free quotes from 20+ vetted BMW specialists today.

mainEntity.name: How much does a BMW X1 E84 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned E84 engine typically costs £2,000–£7,000 supply only [BMW-QUOTE], depending on the engine — the N46 and N20 four-cylinders sit at £2,000–£5,000, the N52 straight-six at £3,000–£5,500, the N47 diesel at £3,200–£6,000, and the N55 turbo-six at £4,500–£7,000. Fitting adds £520–£1,040. Use our calculator for a personalised figure.

mainEntity.name: Which E84 engine is the most reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The N52B30 (xDrive28i, 2009–2011) is the E84's most reliable engine — a 3.0-litre naturally-aspirated straight-six with no turbocharger or high-pressure fuel pump complexity. It's smooth, refined, and has a proven track record. The N20 four-cylinder is also reliable, but watch for timing chain guides on early cars. The N47 diesel has the well-documented timing chain risk.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the BMW X1 E84?

acceptedAnswer.text: The N47 diesel suffers from rear-mounted timing chain stretch — cold-start rattle is the warning sign. The N20 turbo four (2011–2015) has timing chain guide wear, particularly on early cars. The N55 (xDrive35i) has HPFP and VANOS issues. The N52 (2009–2011 xDrive28i) is robust but cooling system failures are common. All diesels require DPF maintenance with urban use.

mainEntity.name: Is the BMW X1 E84 reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: It depends heavily on the engine. The N52 straight-six is genuinely robust — many examples exceed 150,000 miles. The N20 is good but timing chain guides need attention on early cars. The N47 diesel is the biggest risk — timing chain failure is well-documented and expensive. Regular maintenance is critical; these are now 10+ year old cars.

mainEntity.name: Which E84 engine should I avoid?

acceptedAnswer.text: Pre-2012 N47 diesel (18d/20d/23d) without documented timing chain replacement — the rear-mounted chain issue is a known weakness. Early N20 petrol (2011–2013) without chain history is also a risk. An xDrive35i without HPFP and VANOS history is expensive to maintain. Avoid any engine without full service history.

mainEntity.name: How do I know if my E84 engine needs replacing?

acceptedAnswer.text: Cold-start rattle (N47 or N20 timing chain issues), long cranking or limp mode (N55 HPFP), overheating (N52 cooling system), or persistent warning lights are the key warning signs. Use our diagnostic calculator for a personalised assessment.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing an E84 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on the car's value. Against a £7,000 xDrive20d, a £3,200–£5,500 N47 recon is 46–79% — at the upper end, that brushes our 70% BMW scrap threshold. Against a £5,000 sDrive18i, a £2,000–£3,500 N46 recon is 40–70% — borderline. For an xDrive35i at £6,000+, a £4,500–£7,000 N55 recon is 75–117% — a serious decision. Value the car first; if it's a pre-2012 N47 without chain history, walk away.

mainEntity.name: How long do E84 engines last?

acceptedAnswer.text: The N52 straight-six can exceed 150,000 miles with proper servicing — the cooling system is the limiting factor. The N20 is capable of high mileage but timing chain guides need attention. The N47 can reach 120,000+ miles but the timing chain is the limiting factor — addressed proactively, it can last. The N55 is capable of high mileage but HPFP and VANOS work should be budgeted for.

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between the E84, F48, and U11 X1 generations?

acceptedAnswer.text: The E84 (2009–2015) is the first-generation X1 — based on the E90 3 Series platform, it was rear-wheel drive (sDrive) or all-wheel drive (xDrive). The F48 (2015–2022) is the second generation — built on the front-wheel-drive UKL platform shared with the MINI Countryman. The U11 (2022–present) is the third generation — also front-wheel-drive based but with electric and plug-in hybrid options. The E84 is the only X1 with rear-wheel-drive dynamics.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my E84 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation; the engine code is also stamped on the cylinder block. For petrol: N46B20 (sDrive18i), N20B16A (sDrive16i), N20B20 (sDrive20i/xDrive20i/sDrive28i/xDrive28i), N52B30 (xDrive28i 2009–2011), N55B30 (xDrive35i). For diesel: N47D20C (18d/20d), N47D20D (xDrive23d), N47N (sDrive16d). Tell us your variant and registration year and we'll identify it — 20+ vetted BMW specialists will confirm it for free.

hero.h1: BMW X1 E84 Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the X1 E84 — from the N46 naturally-aspirated four and the N47 diesel to the N20 turbocharged four, the N52 straight-six, and the N55 turbo-six that topped the range. Real UK replacement costs and honest reliability data for BMW's first-generation compact SUV. Backed by 120+ real UK enquiries in 2025 [BMW-VERIFIED].

engineDatabase.h2: BMW X1 E84 Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the X1 E84 specifically — including the naturally-aspirated and turbocharged four-cylinders, the straight-six petrols, and the full range of diesel options.

confidenceScore.title: How confident are these ratings?
confidenceScore.text: All ratings are provisional — no dedicated Engine Page exists for any E84 engine family. The N52 straight-six is widely regarded as the most robust petrol option, with the N20 turbo four also proving reliable (though timing chain guides are a known concern on early cars). The N47 diesel carries the well-documented timing chain risk that affects many BMW diesels of this era. The N55 in the xDrive35i adds complexity and cost but delivers impressive performance.

overview.intro: The E84 is the first generation of the BMW X1, produced from October 2009 to June 2015. Built on the E90 3 Series platform, it brought rear-wheel-drive dynamics to the compact SUV segment and was the first X model available with sDrive (rear-wheel drive). The engine line-up evolved significantly during production: early cars offered the N46 naturally-aspirated four, the N52 straight-six, and the N47 diesel four. A 2012 facelift introduced the N20 turbocharged four-cylinder and the N55 turbo-six, while the N47 received updates. The E84 was replaced by the front-wheel-drive F48 X1 in 2015.
overview.keyFacts: Platform: BMW E84 (based on E90 3 Series, front-engine, rear-wheel drive / xDrive) • Predecessor: None (X1 is a new model line) • Successor: BMW X1 (F48) • Sibling body styles in this generation: 5-door SUV only
overview.marketIntelligenceLine: The X1 E84 generated 120 real UK engine enquiries in 2025 [BMW-VERIFIED], with the N47 diesel variants accounting for the majority of demand based on market patterns.

items.quote: The 2.0-litre turbocharged four delivers 184 bhp — enough for everyday driving with excellent fuel economy. It replaced the N52 in the xDrive28i with 245 bhp in later years. It's the sensible choice that still feels quick, and it's cheaper to run than the six-cylinders.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want modern turbo performance with sensible running costs.

items.quote: The N47's rear-mounted timing chain is the E84's defining risk — cold-start rattle is the warning, and a snapped chain means full replacement. Early cars (2009–2011) are the highest risk. BMW revised the chain design in later years, but the issue was never fully eliminated.
items.whoItsFor: only buyers with documented chain-replacement history or post-2012 cars.

items.quote: The 3.0-litre turbo straight-six delivers 306 bhp of effortless performance, but it's complex and costly to maintain. High-pressure fuel pump failures, VANOS issues, and oil leaks are well-documented. A full engine replacement can exceed £7,000.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want the most powerful E84 and are prepared for the running costs.

items.quote: The 1.6-litre turbo four is often overlooked — it was introduced late in the E84's life (2013) primarily for tax-sensitive markets. With 143 bhp, it's the entry-level petrol, but it's a surprisingly capable and economical engine for urban use.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want a petrol E84 with the lowest possible running costs.

rows.ownershipVerdict: Late Entry — budget for chain work
Most Refined — the one to buy
High Power, High Cost — budget for maintenance
Economy Diesel — improved but still a risk

economicsRule.title: The E84 rule
economicsRule.text: A £3,200–£5,500 N47 recon against a £4,000–£10,000 X1 sits at 32–138% of the car's value — on a sub‑£5,000 example, that brushes our 70% BMW scrap threshold. Against a well‑kept £7,000+ example, the same recon drops to 46–79% — borderline but often viable for a cherished car. For an N52 xDrive28i at £5,000–£8,000, a £3,000–£5,500 recon is 38–110% — a serious decision. Value the car first; if it's a pre‑2012 N47 without chain history, walk away or budget for the work immediately.

keyTakeaways.question: Which E84 Costs Least To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: N46B20 (sDrive18i) — simple naturally-aspirated four, no turbo, no high-pressure fuel pump.

keyTakeaways.question: Which E84 Costs Most To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: N55B30 (xDrive35i) — complex turbo six, HPFP and VANOS issues, expensive parts.

keyTakeaways.question: Which E84 Is Best Value?
keyTakeaways.answer: N52B30 (xDrive28i) — 258 bhp straight-six, proven reliability, and often cheaper than the turbocharged alternatives.

problems.title: N47 Timing Chain Stretch (Diesel Engines)
problems.recommendation: At the first sign of cold-start rattle, get the chain inspected immediately — a £650–£1,800 preventative repair is dramatically cheaper than the £3,200+ replacement that follows if ignored. On an E84 worth under £5,000, this decision alone determines whether the car stays economically viable.

problems.title: N20 Timing Chain Guide Wear (Petrol Four-Cylinders)
problems.recommendation: If you're buying an N20-powered E84, look for documented timing chain work or budget £1,200–£2,500 immediately — especially on pre-2013 cars. A preventative chain and tensioner replacement is dramatically cheaper than the £2,500+ replacement that follows a failure.

problems.title: N55 HPFP & VANOS Failure (xDrive35i)
problems.recommendation: If your xDrive35i develops long cranking or rough running, have the HPFP inspected immediately — a £800–£1,800 repair is cheaper than the £4,500+ replacement that follows a catastrophic failure.

problems.title: N52 Cooling System & Valve Lift (xDrive28i)
problems.recommendation: If you're buying an N52-powered E84, budget for a cooling system refresh at 80,000 miles — £500–£1,200 is cheap insurance against a £3,000+ replacement.

replacementCosts.h2: X1 E84 Engine Replacement Costs — By Engine and Condition

coreVariants.scopeNote: All variants listed are E84 only. sDrive denotes rear-wheel drive; xDrive denotes all-wheel drive. The xDrive28i used the N52B30 straight-six from 2009–2011 and the N20B20 turbo four from 2011–2015. The xDrive35i with the N55B30 turbo six was introduced in 2012.
